text: This is a laparoscopic removal of inguinal hernia mesh placed during a robotic tap hernia repair in a patient with chronic pain. An eighteen-year-old healthy male presented to an outside hospital with acute onset of left lower quadrant abdominal pain. Extensive workup including CT and lab work did not reveal a source. On CT scan, an incidental small left inguinal hernia was found and he underwent an expertly done robotic tap inguinal hernia repair with mesh. Postoperatively, the pain continued to worsen. He developed systemic symptoms as well as psychological distress. Another extensive workup failed to reveal the cause of these symptoms.

The patient presented to our office requesting mesh removal after three months of worsening symptoms. We had a thorough discussion of the risks of mesh removal, including ongoing groin pain and systemic symptoms, bleeding, infection, numbness, testicular pain or chitis or ischemia, visceral and vascular injury, recurrent or new hernia formation, and bowel obstruction.

During the laparoscopic excision of the mesh, the surgeon identifies an inferior folded edge of the mesh. The examination of the abdominal wall shows no scar tissue or spigellian hernia at the patient's indicated area of pain. Exploration of the posterior rectus fascia reveals no visible pathology such as neuroma or intramuscular lipoma.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision laterally and inferiorly to create a flap, exposing the inguinal region and the mesh. Monopolar cautery is used to maintain hemostasis, allowing clear visualization of the anatomical planes and structures.

The surgeon exposes the inferior edge of the mesh with a second peritoneal incision, connecting it to the previous incision to complete the flap. The dissection of the mesh is carried medially using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, with electrocautery applied as needed.

The surgeon mobilizes the inferior clam-shelled edge of the mesh from the myobactinial orifice, encountering a cord lipoma. The lipoma is excised as a potential source of pain. The vas deferens and cord structures are identified and carefully mobilized free from the mesh using sharp dissection.
